proposal.reasoning: This is the first iteration in the exploration phase with no prior data. I am proposing 8 diverse parameter sets to broadly sample the parameter space and identify promising regions. The strategy focuses on: (1) Baseline middle values, (2-3) High vs low cohesion (epsilon variations) to test effects on density and HVap, (4-5) Small vs large size (rmin variations) to test packing effects, (6-7) High vs low dipole moments (charge and bond length variations) to test effects on dipole and surface tension, and (8) A balanced combination of moderately favorable values. This covers the primary axes of variation that affect the target thermodynamic and electronic properties.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Changing epsilon (cohesion strength) from weak (-0.3) to strong (-1.1) will modulate density and heat of vaporization because stronger van der Waals attractions lead to tighter packing and higher energy required for vaporization. Changing rmin (size) from small (2.0) to large (2.6) will control packing density - smaller beads allow higher densities. Changing dummy bead charges (0.15 to 0.45) and bond lengths (0.4 to 1.0 Å) will directly control the molecular dipole moment (μ = q × d), affecting surface tension and dielectric properties.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: High epsilon values should increase density, heat of vaporization, and surface tension. Small rmin values should increase density. High dipole configurations should increase surface tension and match the target dipole moment of 3.72 D. This systematic exploration will reveal which combination of these factors best reproduces the target properties (density ~0.936, HVap ~10.95, ST ~32.43, Dipole ~3.72).
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating all 8 parameter sets in parallel molecular dynamics simulations and comparing the resulting thermodynamic and electronic properties against experimental targets.

proposal.reasoning: The previous iteration resulted in catastrophic crashes across all forks due to extreme instabilities: atoms moving >1200 Å/fs, pressures of 4660-5436 bar, and explosive behavior. The diagnostic identifies the root causes as: (1) excessive dummy bead charges (±0.3) creating strong electrostatic forces, (2) overly stiff harmonic constraints (300 kcal/mol/Å²) causing high-frequency numerical instabilities, and (3) insufficient excluded volume leading to atomic overlaps. Since we're in the exploration phase with complete simulation failure, I must make significant, physically-motivated adjustments to stabilize the system while preserving the target dipole moment. The strategy varies: (1) DUP/DUN charges from 0.15-0.22 (reduced from 0.3), (2) dummy bond force constants from 110-150 (just above the 100 minimum, reduced from 300), (3) dummy bond lengths from 0.82-1.08 Å (increased from 0.7 to compensate charge reduction and maintain dipole moment μ=q×d), (4) angle force constants from 15-20 (reduced from 25 to increase flexibility), and (5) LJ sigma (rminby2) from 2.35-2.6 Å (increased from 2.3 to provide more excluded volume and prevent overlaps). The 8 forks systematically explore combinations of these stabilizing changes to identify a viable parameter region.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Reducing dummy bead charges from ±0.3 to ±0.15-0.22 and softening the harmonic constraints from 300 to 110-150 kcal/mol/Å² will eliminate the extreme forces causing atomic explosions (pressure >5000 bar) and high-velocity instabilities. To preserve the target dipole moment of 3.72 D despite lower charges, we increase the bond lengths from 0.7 to 0.82-1.08 Å (dipole μ = q × d). Reducing angle force constants from 25 to 15-20 kcal/mol/rad² allows the molecular geometry to flexibly accommodate the dummy beads without strain. Increasing LJ sigma (rminby2) from 2.3 to 2.35-2.6 Å increases excluded volume, preventing initial atomic overlaps that contributed to the pressure spikes.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This will stabilize the molecular dynamics simulation by eliminating the 'explosive' behavior, allowing the system to reach equilibrium and produce valid thermodynamic properties (density, heat of vaporization, surface tension) rather than crash placeholders (10000.0). Success is defined by simulations completing without velocity crashes (v < 1200 Å/fs) and producing physically reasonable density (~0.9 g/cm³) and pressure values, indicating a stable liquid state.

The strategy focuses on: (1) Reducing LJ epsilon magnitudes from -0.55 toward -0.35 to -0.45 to decrease intermolecular attraction and thus HVap/density; (2) Increasing rminby2 values toward their maxima (2.8 for CNI, 2.7 for MOC) to increase excluded volume and further reduce density; (3) Maximizing dummy bead separation by extending bond lengths to 2.0-2.4 Å (upper boundary) and opening valence angles toward 165° to maximize dipole moment geometrically (μ = q × d), allowing charge reduction for stability; (4) Setting harmonic force constants to the boundary minimum (100) to soften vibrations as recommended. hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Reducing epsilon magnitudes from -0.55 toward -0.35 to -0.45 decreases the Lennard-Jones potential well depth, directly reducing excessive intermolecular cohesion responsible for the 140% HVap overestimation. Simultaneously increasing rminby2 (sigma/2) from 2.35 to 2.5-2.8 Å increases excluded volume, reducing density toward the target 0.936 g/cm³. To maintain the target dipole moment (3.72 D) while reducing electrostatic instabilities, we maximize the geometric separation between DUP and DUN dummy beads by extending bond lengths to 2.0-2.4 Å (approaching the 2.4 Å boundary) and opening the DUP-CNI-MOC and CNI-MOC-DUN angles toward 165° (near-linear configuration), which maximizes the charge separation distance d in μ = q × d. This geometric compensation allows us to test lower charge magnitudes (0.10-0.14) for improved stability while maintaining dipole accuracy.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This will reduce the heat of vaporization from 26.1 kcal/mol toward the target 11.0 kcal/mol and decrease density from 0.980 toward 0.936 g/cm³ by weakening excessive LJ attractions and increasing excluded volume. The extended geometry will maintain or improve dipole moment accuracy toward 3.72 D despite charge reduction, while kb=100 ensures sufficient flexibility to prevent velocity crashes seen with stiffer springs.

proposal.reasoning: The diagnostic report indicates that the current best parameters (f=28.68) suffer from systematically overestimated cohesive properties: surface tension (+21%), density (+2%), and heat of vaporization (+2.8%). This pattern indicates excessive Lennard-Jones epsilon values and/or insufficient excluded volume. The optimization is in the exploration phase with recent improvements (stuck_counter=0), so I propose 8 forks that systematically explore the recommended corrections: (1) reducing epsilon magnitudes to weaken cohesion (addressing surface tension and HVap), (2) increasing Rmin values to reduce packing efficiency (addressing density and surface tension), (3) slightly reducing dummy charges from 0.18 to 0.17-0.175 (further reducing surface tension while maintaining dipole), and (4) testing angle variations (155-160°) to balance dipole moment with geometric stability. All forks maintain dummy bond lengths ≤1.0 Å as warned by the diagnostic to avoid velocity overflow crashes. The forks range from conservative adjustments (Fork 1) to aggressive epsilon reduction (Fork 7), aggressive Rmin increases (Fork 3, 8), and combined parameter shifts (Fork 6) to map the sensitivity landscape and identify the optimal trade-off between surface tension correction and dipole moment preservation.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Reducing epsilon magnitudes (e.g., -0.5 → -0.42 to -0.48) weakens the attractive Lennard-Jones interactions, directly reducing surface tension (excess energy at the liquid-vapor interface) and heat of vaporization (energy required to overcome cohesive forces). Simultaneously increasing Rmin values (by 2-5%) increases excluded volume, reducing packing efficiency and lowering density while further decreasing surface tension. Slightly reducing dummy charges from ±0.18 to ±0.17 reduces electrostatic contributions to surface tension without significantly compromising the dipole moment (μ = q × d), especially when compensated by maintaining wide valence angles (155-160°). Keeping dummy bond lengths at or below 1.0 Å ensures simulation stability by preventing angular momentum instabilities that cause velocity overflows.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This will reduce the surface tension deviation from +21% toward the target value, correct the slight density overestimation (+2%), and fine-tune the heat of vaporization, while maintaining the dipole moment within acceptable error bounds. The systematic variation across 8 forks will identify the optimal balance between cohesive force reduction and electrostatic property preservation.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating all 8 parameter sets in parallel, monitoring for simulation stability (ensuring no velocity crashes or dummy bead instabilities), and measuring the resulting surface tension, density, heat of vaporization, and dipole moment to identify which combination of epsilon reductions and Rmin increases yields the best overall score.
